perturbation.original_text: 13.2 The express provisions of this Agreement are in place of corresponding warranties, conditions, terms, undertakings and obligations implied by statute, common law, custom, trade usage, course of dealing or otherwise (including implied undertakings of satisfactory quality, conformity with description and reasonable fitness for purpose), all of which are hereby excluded to the maximum extent permitted by law.
perturbation.changed_text: 13.2 The express provisions of this Agreement are in place of corresponding warranties, conditions, terms, undertakings and obligations implied by statute, common law, custom, trade usage, course of dealing or otherwise (excluding implied undertakings of satisfactory quality, conformity with description and reasonable fitness for purpose), all of which are hereby excluded to the maximum extent permitted by law.
perturbation.explanation: By removing "including" from the text and instead saying "excluding", the conformity with description and reasonable fitness for purpose are removed. By removing this essential part, which in most locations is covered by Sale of Goods Act and the implied warranty of merchantability, the contract is non-compliant.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Sale of Goods Act 1979 s 14 implies terms as to satisfactory quality and fitness for purpose. The modified text attempts to exclude these implied terms, but under UK law, particularly when dealing with consumers, such exclusions are often restricted or unenforceable by the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977.

Accept and close Share this: Facebook Twitter Reddit LinkedIn WhatsApp Act Summary Introduction This summary examines the Sale of Goods Act 1979 (“the Act”) within its context: why it was drafted, what are its important provisions, and how it has changed since it came into force. It is submitted that the Sale of Goods Act 1979 has been part of a change in consumer dealings, with its most significant contributions being to the rights consumers have where they buy products that turn out to be faulty. Indeed, its use has been so central to implied terms in particular, that one author has been criticised for failing to see the contribution of the Act to the law of implied terms. [1] At the same time, the Act’s importance has to an extent been diminished by the introduction of very recent legislation. [2] Rationale for Drafting The Act codified various provisions, such as the formation of contract, which was already prevalent and well-established in common law. [3] However, its contribution was to enhance consumer confidence. It has been argued that without the Act, and its successors, there would be little protection for consumers. [4] This imbalance of consumer rights would lead to significant caution, and even “defensive consumerism.” [5] If consumers believe they have few rights when purchasing goods, then they are slow to trust newer and less-familiar brands, and will continue to buy goods from established brands, even where those goods actually lack in quality. Therefore, if the legislature does not guarantee certain minimums for consumer confidence, compet
perturbation.justification: The modified text, by changing "including" to "excluding", attempts to remove the implied terms of satisfactory quality, conformity with description, and reasonable fitness for purpose. The Law Explanation states that the Sale of Goods Act 1979 implies terms as to satisfactory quality and fitness for purpose, and the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977 may restrict exclusions of these implied terms, especially for consumers. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references the Sale of Goods Act 1979 and its contribution to consumer rights regarding faulty products and implied terms. This removal of consumer protection creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 15. TERMINATION 15.1 Either Party may (without prejudice to its other rights) terminate this Agreement at any time by giving written notice to the other Party if: 15.1.1 the other Party becomes unable to pay its debts (within the meaning of section 123 (l)(e) or (2) of the Insolvency Act 1986), admits its inability to pay its debts or becomes insolvent, or (ii) a petition is presented, an order made or a resolution passed for the liquidation (otherwise than for the purposes of a solvent amalgamation or reconstruction), administration, bankruptcy or dissolution of the other Party, or (iii) an administrative or other receiver, manager, trustee, liquidator, administrator or similar person or officer is appointed to the other Party and/or over all or any part of the assets of the other Party, or (iv) the other Party enters into or proposes any composition or arrangement concerning its debts with its creditors (or any class of its creditors) generally, or (v) anything equivalent to any of the events or circumstances stated in (i) to (iv) inclusive occurs in any applicable jurisdiction; or 15.1.2 the other Party commits a material or persistent breach of the provisions of this Agreement (a "Breach"), provided that if the Breach is remediable, the other Party shall be given thirty (30) days to remedy the Breach from receipt of the first Party's written notice specifying the nature of the Breach and requesting that the same be remedied.
perturbation.changed_text: 15. TERMINATION 15.1 Either Party may (without prejudice to its other rights) terminate this Agreement at any time by giving written notice to the other Party if: 15.1.1 the other Party becomes unable to pay its debts (within the meaning of section 123 (l)(e) or (2) of the Insolvency Act 1986), admits its inability to pay its debts or becomes insolvent, or (ii) a petition is presented, an order made or a resolution passed for the liquidation (otherwise than for the purposes of a solvent amalgamation or reconstruction), administration, bankruptcy or dissolution of the other Party, or (iii) an administrative or other receiver, manager, trustee, liquidator, administrator or similar person or officer is appointed to the other Party and/or over all or any part of the assets of the other Party, or (iv) the other Party enters into or proposes any composition or arrangement concerning its debts with its creditors (or any class of its creditors) generally, or (v) anything equivalent to any of the events or circumstances stated in (i) to (iv) inclusive occurs in any applicable jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the text '15.1.2 the other Party commits a material or persistent breach of the provisions of this Agreement (a "Breach"), provided that if the Breach is remediable, the other Party shall be given thirty (30) days to remedy the Breach from receipt of the first Party's written notice specifying the nature of the Breach and requesting that the same be remedied.', the clause only allows for termination in the circumstances of insolvency. A material or persistent breach is grounds for termination under common law, thus its removal is detrimental.
perturbation.contradicted_law: English common law principles of contract law
perturbation.law_explanation: Under English common law, a material breach of contract typically entitles the non-breaching party to terminate the contract. By removing the clause that allows termination for a material or persistent breach, the agreement contradicts the common law principle that provides such a right, potentially restricting a party's ability to terminate the agreement when the other party significantly fails to perform its obligations.

You are here: BAILII >> Databases >> England and Wales Court of Appeal (Civil Division) Decisions >> Sun Bank Plc v Wootten & Anor [2004] EWCA Civ 1423 (15 October 2004) URL: https://www.bailii.org/ew/cases/EWCA/Civ/2004/1423.html Cite as: [2004] EWCA Civ 1423 [ New search ] [ Context ] [ View without highlighting ] [ Printable RTF version ] [ Help ] Neutral Citation Number: [2004] EWCA Civ 1423 B2/2004/0460(A) IN THE SUPREME COURT OF JUDICATURE IN THE COURT OF APPEAL (CIVIL DIVISION) ON APPEAL FROM THE MEDWAY COUNTY COURT (MR RECORDER SPON-SMITH) Royal Courts of Justice Strand London, WC2 15th October 2004 B e f o r e : LORD JUSTICE BUXTON LORD JUSTICE SEDLEY ____________________ SUN BANK PLC Respondents/Claimants -v- (1) ROGER PATRICK WOOTTEN First Defendant (2) MARY ELLEN WOOTTEN Second Defendant/Appellant ____________________ (Computer-Aided Transcript of the Stenograph Notes of Smith Bernal Wordwave Limited 190 Fleet Street, London EC4A 2AG Tel No: 020 7404 1400 Fax No: 020 7831 8838 Official Shorthand Writers to the Court) ____________________ MR M SEFTON (instructed by Weil Gotshal & Manges) appeared on behalf of the Appellant MR EYRE (instructed by Eversheds) appeared on behalf of the Respondents MR R WOOTTEN appeared IN PERSON as an Interested Party ____________________ HTML VERSION OF JUDGMENT ____________________ Crown Copyright © LORD JUSTICE BUXTON: This is an appeal from a decision of Mr Recorder Spon-Smith in the Medway County Court. Judgment was handed down on 9th February 2004, the hearing having taken place in the previous December. That part of the chronology has some peripheral reference to the issues before this court. I should say straight away that no criticism is made in this appeal of the judgment of the Learned Recorder on the evidence as it stood before him. It is right that that is so, because I take the liberty of saying that the Recorder dealt with the case clearly and concisely and in a way that could not possibly be challenged. The appeal turns on a question of fresh evidence and is effectively an application that the court should admit fresh evidence and, on the basis of that order, a re-trial. In order to understand why the fresh evidence matters and its relation to the previous proceedings, it is necessary to give some account of the trial and of the findings made by the judge; even though, as I say, those in themselves are not an issue. The case was concerned with a claim for possession by the claimants, now called Sun Bank Plc, although they previously had a different name or incarnation, for possession of premises at Rochester Road in Burham, Near Rochester, Kent. The first and second defendants were Mr Roger Patrick Wootten and his then, and as I believe still current, wife Mrs Mary Ellen Wootten, the joint legal owners of the property. The claim for possession was founded on a legal charge dated 15th March 1990 ("the mortgage"). There was no challenge that the mortgage had been executed by Mr Wootten. It purported to have been executed by Mrs Wootten also, although the issue in the trial before the judge, and before us, was whether indeed she had so signed the mortgage. The outstanding debt was very considerable. At the time of the trial it was nearly £200,000, and is no doubt more at the present date, there being a very h
perturbation.justification: The original contract text includes a clause (15.1.2) that allows termination if the other party commits a material breach, provided they are given 30 days to remedy the breach. The changed text removes this clause. The Law Explanation states that under English common law, a material breach entitles the non-breaching party to terminate the contract. By removing the clause, the changed text contradicts the common law principle, restricting a party's ability to terminate for a significant failure of performance. Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a case law example, demonstrating the existence of legal proceedings regarding contract disputes, adding external validity to the premise of termination clauses. The removal of 15.1.2 directly eliminates a party's right to terminate, which is contradictory to the general principle described in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: 11. CONFIDENTIALITY 11.1 For purposes of this Clause: Authorised Persons means the directors, employees, officers, professional advisers, agents and contractors of each Party; Confidential Information means all information in any medium or format (written, oral, visual or electronic, and whether or not marked or described as "confidential"), together with Copies, which relates to a Party (the "Disclosing Party"), to its Group, or to its (or its Group members') employees, officers, customers or suppliers, and which is directly or indirectly disclosed by the Disclosing Party to the other Party (the "Recipient Party") in the course of its dealings relating to this Agreement, before or after the date of this Agreement. However, the following information is not "Confidential Information" for the purposes of this Agreement: (i) information which is in the public domain other than as a result of breach of this Agreement or any separate confidentiality undertaking between the Parties; (ii) information which the Recipient Party received, free of any obligation of confidence, from a third party which itself was not under any obligation of confidence in relation to that information; and 9 (iii) information which was developed or created independently by or on behalf of the Recipient Party or any member of the Recipient Party's Group; and Copies means all reproductions (hard copy or electronic), extracts, summaries or analyses of Confidential Information in any medium or format made by or on behalf of any Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. CONFIDENTIALITY 11.1 For purposes of this Clause: Authorised Persons means the directors, employees, officers, professional advisers, agents and contractors of each Party; Confidential Information means all information in any medium or format (written, oral, visual or electronic, and whether or not marked or described as "confidential"), together with Copies, which relates to a Party (the "Disclosing Party"), to its Group, or to its (or its Group members') employees, officers, customers or suppliers, and which is directly or indirectly disclosed by the Disclosing Party to the other Party (the "Recipient Party") in the course of its dealings relating to this Agreement, before or after the date of this Agreement. However, the following information is not "Confidential Information" for the purposes of this Agreement: (i) information which is in the public domain other than as a result of breach of this Agreement or any separate confidentiality undertaking between the Parties; (ii) information which the Recipient Party received, free of any obligation of confidence, from a third party which itself was not under any obligation of confidence in relation to that information; and Copies means all reproductions (hard copy or electronic), extracts, summaries or analyses of Confidential Information in any medium or format made by or on behalf of any Party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing ' (iii) information which was developed or created independently by or on behalf of the Recipient Party or any member of the Recipient Party's Group;', it means that information independently created by the Recipient Party is still considered confidential. This is unduly restrictive and could be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade, especially if such independent creation was based on general knowledge and not specifically derived from the Disclosing Party's confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: English common law principles of restraint of trade
perturbation.law_citation: Restraint of Trade Doctrine - Common Law
perturbation.law_explanation: The English common law doctrine of restraint of trade generally voids agreements that unreasonably restrict a person's ability to carry on their trade or profession. By classifying independently developed information as confidential, the modified agreement could unduly restrict the Recipient Party's ability to use its own knowledge and skills, thereby potentially violating principles against unreasonable restraint of trade.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: restraint of trade | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you restraint of trade A restraint of trade is any activity that tends to limit a party's ability to enter into transactions. The term is most commonly used in the context of government antitrust regulation. For example, federally, 15 U.S.C. § 1 prohibits “[e]very contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations.” For example, in American Needle, Inc. v. Nat’l Football League , the U.S. Supreme Court held that licensing activities for individual teams’ intellectual property conducted through a corporation separate from the teams with its own management [i.e. the NFL] fell within a possible restraint of trade covered under 15 U.S.C. § 1. Many states also have their own local restraints on anti-competitive business activity. For example, Massachusetts General Law Ch. 93 § 4 states “[e]very contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce in the commonwealth shall be unlawful.” [Last reviewed in April of 2025 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities unfair competition wex definitions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified contract omits a clause stating that information independently developed by the Recipient Party is not considered confidential. The Perturbation Explanation states this omission could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint of trade. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that classifying independently developed information as confidential could unduly restrict the Recipient Party's ability to use its own knowledge and skills, potentially violating principles against unreasonable restraint of trade. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'restraint of trade' as 'any activity that tends to limit a party's ability to enter into transactions.' By classifying independently developed information as confidential, the modified contract limits the Recipient Party's ability to use that information, thus creating a possible restraint of trade.
